speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six. Today is June 10, 1996, and our survivor is Shoshana Kahn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, those guys get very annoyed with me, with my so-called chutzpah, which is called [GERMAN] in German. They just yelled at me all the time, that something is wrong with me, and they're going to really show me the-- the ropes at room 70-something. [BACKROUND NOISE] It think it was 79.

speaker: interviewer
text: Off I went to 79, and what did [? Catherine ?] do in those situation? She did-- she preempted. Before the guy could say something, I already said it. This is-- I said, what is wrong with those people out there? All afternoon, they have nothing better to do than to talk with me and harass me, me, Veronica [? Reimers? ?] I mean, did we win the war already? That was always a like, did we win the war already? And that guy was on the phone, he turned around, and he laughed. He said, [GERMAN], which is very tasty, delicious, delicious, he yelled. And he complained. Can you imagine, he said, that I have all day long, all week long, to deal with you [GERMAN], which means those stupid people who are not intelligent, and stupid, uneducated people? Would you like to be in my place, he said. You have something to complain? And then he said, [GERMAN], Fraulein, what is it I can do for you? I said, first of all, I want [GERMAN]-- that is bread-- bread coupons. [SPEAKING GERMAN], what other [GERMAN], and a [? pass, ?] please. [SPEAKING GERMAN] Fraulein, of course. Fraulein, Fraulein. He couldn't have been nicer to me. I mean, absolutely, here I come out of [GERMAN]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I got all those doo-dads in my hand, and I walked through the main office, and I howl-- and I yelled, auf Wiedersehen. And he gave me an-- an guard, somebody to accompany me to a place where they collect people who have no homes, so because I am a [GERMAN] from Germany. And he went down the steps. I have mentioned that before that, they took me to the bathroom and looked into all my orifices and what have you, couldn't find anything, and kept on saying, don't look-- and that woman who did it says to me, don't look anybody in the eye, don't look them in the eye. All right, what-- OK, so we walk down. He's about to open the door. I said, one minute, please. I scampered up those steps, went back to the office, and yelled, not auf Wiedersehen. I don't want to see you again any more in my life. And that guy got blue in his face-- blue, I tell you. His eyes were bulging. He kept on knocking the table and said, once more, I see your chutzpah face, your [GERMAN], that will be the end of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I hopped down the steps, I thought I was such a big hero. And that guard took me, and hit me, and hit me, and hit me, and I [INAUDIBLE] He said, you stupid goat, you stupid cow, aren't you lucky you got out, away from those [? 'brothers,' ?] quote unquote? You don't know what we went through, watching you at this interrogation, how be worried. And you have the gall to go back up there? I'm not talking to you again. [GERMAN]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he marched me to his school. He never even said goodbye to me. [INAUDIBLE] unforgivable. And then the school was full of people who were so-called [GERMAN], all sleeping on the floor, and then I got the runs-- then it worked. And I had all night to step on all those people, back and forth, and back and forth, and back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, the next morning, I made my way back to the-- to this lovely family, the Poles, who came running out of the house, and kissing me, and hugging me, and being so happy, and they got to cook me a big feast, and everybody was surrounding me and applauding me, how smart I was. And one day, it was one day or two days, there came a [INAUDIBLE] or-- or a Jeep, or whatever those guys are driving-- the soldiers-- and said, is there a Fraulein Veronica [? Reimers ?] living? That she is. Now, I have to come with them, and I have a job to do for the-- for the-- for the Greeks-- for the Greek. And they-- I'll never forget this lovely lady. She-- I didn't have a coat. She went to the closet, undid her good coat, her Sunday coat, which was an imitation fur. Can you imagine what that cost in those days? She had wrapped in papers. She lovingly unwrapped it and puts the coat around me-- that coat for the winter. What time of year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter. It was January-- or what? It was the end-- it was December-- it couldn't have been. It was that type of-- time of year. At Danzig-- wow, was that cold-- I mean, snow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you wear on your feet?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave me shoes. Those guys dressed-- dressed me up. I mean, they had stuff for me. They dressed me up. I did-- they did-- I didn't have the uniform anymore, not my clogs. I had stockings and things. I mean, those people took good care of me. They took very good care for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, then-- then I got in the car, and Fraulein Veronica [? Reimer ?] got the job with Organisation Todt. I got assigned an apartment, had to appear in an office every morning and say, heil Hitler. And there was not much to do, because it was over for the Germans. I mean, it was just pushing the papers and the pen back and forth. It was all a big show for nothing. I do remember Hitler's birthday, his last. It was, I think, the 8th of-- it was in April-- April the 15-- April something, Hitler's birthday. And we had to come all down there and say heil Hitler, and all those lovely thing one says. But the other thing, which was-- which very interesting was that I, as a German woman, was-- had access to radio and news. The others were not allowed. There were lots of Polish [GERMAN] and Russian [GERMAN]. They have always those-- they had everything compartmentalized there. That was a [GERMAN], that was not a [GERMAN]. I was the only true German.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE] I always have to think that. And I-- I got to listen to the radio, and I know that the Russians are here, and the Americans are there, and here's a freed camp, and there's a freed camp, and there is little Shoshama Rose in-- in the middle with the Germans, and cannot budge. Did anyone know your true identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of. There was-- the thing was-- thing was that they made me the Girl Friday of-- you know, every organization had one SS person, and I was his so-called Girl Friday. What did I do? I brought him the coffee, and then the papers, and then the aftershave, and we-- and then we always-- we were [GERMAN], what they called-- surrounded by-- by enemies. So we always were on the move. And then we were on the move, I was driving this SS as a German lady in a taxi.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you talk to them about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked about-- with whom? Depends with whom? I mean, once I kind-- I kind of-- there was one very nasty guy who took advantage of the Polish girls, and he was saying something to me, and I said, well, [INAUDIBLE] relative, my Herr. [SPEAKING GERMAN] All right, how do you know those words-- relative? And at one point, they had a discussion about film. And you know my [INAUDIBLE] was going, and I had them all fooled, and I was so stupid as I can be, and luckier than anything in the world. I mean, the luck that I had, you wouldn't believe it. And all of a sudden, I burst out with my favorite film, a film from the Soviet Union.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know that film? Very classic-- that's about a-- a-- a-- a American woman in a-- working in a circus, falling in love with a black man, having a mulatto child, and she was scared to death they will find out she has a black child. She kept on hiding it, and hiding it. And then there was beautiful music, da, da, da, which she sings to the baby, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I love that movie. She was a wonderful actress, and then she somehow-- I don't know how she ends up in the Soviet Union, that-- that land of the free. And she-- she's still afraid of the little black baby will be found out, and then comes the old grandfather, and finds that baby. And she's scared to death. Said, darling, he says, you are here in the Soviet Union. You can have black children, you can have green children, you can even have checkered children. We'll love them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was my favorite moment. This was called It's a classic. And here I tell him about and there was quite appall. And that's-- my chef then says to me, Veronica, where do you know those things from? Ha! And I was thinking, what would [? Catherine ?] have said? And [? Catherine ?] said, very fast, that those films have been taken over by the Germans in some town, and they showed it to us as decadence, and [INAUDIBLE], and ba, ba, ba, ba, ba. And there was a sigh of relief all around. And then I said, please watch my tongue. I talk too much, I said to myself. So we were moving about and moving about, and-- and in those times, the-- the soldiers were children-- 15 years old, 14 years old, because they had to bring those in. They didn't have-- the uniforms were far too-- or very old ones, yeah? The uniforms were far too large. The-- the-- the-- the pants were falling down. They're kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was the only Jewish-- not Jewish, German mommy from there. And they used to bomb-- the Russians used to bomb the trains. The train has ammunitions, and then that all will explode, and they had those fancy little words, the air is-- is rich in iron, you know, little jokes. And the kids were screaming in fright. They were so afraid. And they all were screaming, mommy, mommy, mommy. And they all fell on me, And I hear. I see it. And my arms around a whole bunch of little-- little German children crying at my chest. [INAUDIBLE]. Then it all finished. They all dispersed. It was the end. Everybody was on his own. And then you had to-- you had to be-- [INAUDIBLE] was sleeping in bunkers and those things which were [? holed ?] out in the ground. And there was somebody from the office who-- who knew me and saw that they get something to eat, Veronica.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the end of the-- it was the [? 4th. ?] It was May already. I think it was end of April or May, probably end of April, the beginning of May. And then I, and then I found a house, and I went to sleep in that house. Was left. The people left it. It was all alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there I found a jacket. I found a black-- black evening dress. Instead of my uniform-- I had a uniform, mind you. Would you believe it? It was a [INAUDIBLE] on the arm. That was, that was really [INAUDIBLE], cheesy. And I threw that all away and put on whatever I found.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got into trouble. Because there was one guy who tried to make me sleep with him. And he did it in not very, very nice way. He blackmailed me. He said, either you'll come to bed with me or we will-- I will report you as a spy. In those days, they didn't ask much. It mean, they were really hysterical. People were hanging from apple trees [BACKROUND NOISE] and from [? wherever. ?] They just shot each other. I mean, honestly, it was really hysteria. And then he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What nationality was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German, [INAUDIBLE] German SS, some big shot. And I said, OK. I went in, and when I slept in my bed, he was standing by the window and had a big torch and have it [? give ?] me the Chinese torture, with all night shines that thing in my face. And so I shouldn't sleep. I shall break down. And then he had me arrested. And he told this one guy to bring me to the-- to the-- to a court. I mean, they had this kind of kangaroo courts. There was in an SS man who wrote [? on ?] the order. They got [? shots, ?] they got next shot, next shot. It was such a deal. And he was walking. He was a gun, and I next to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he kept on all the time, on my [INAUDIBLE]. It was [INAUDIBLE], what are we going to do? I have a child your age at home, a daughter. And I, I, I don't know what will we do. What [INAUDIBLE]. I said, how about letting me go? Well, he didn't. [INAUDIBLE] No, that he didn't. I mean, that much, he didn't like me. So I said, let's make a deal. You bring me there. That's your order, and that's all your order is, to get me there. The rest, leave to me. And I thought to myself, I will make and do it again. I mean, at the last moment, I will find something. Captain [INAUDIBLE] did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were waiting in line. You know, they were again lining up, walking in. The guy was sitting there, writing out a note, taking out and being processed. That's what's it called, being processed, what is a euphemism for being killed. Then come my, my, my turn. And my poor guard was Snow White and was bibbling. He was shaking so much, he couldn't hold his gun. I had to kick him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And here the guy was standing, writing out the orders. And I see his hands are just covered with eczemas. I mean, the skin was tearing off bloody. And again, I preempted. Before he said something, I said, my leave is [INAUDIBLE]. And whatever his [INAUDIBLE] his thing was. I said, you're suffering of eczemas. So he said, and he looks up [INAUDIBLE]. And what is there to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I suffered of eczemas because I come from Latvia and ate a lot of fish. And I'm allergic to fish. And always had those blisters and [INAUDIBLE]. And I didn't know that's allergy in those days. They used to treat only the symptoms and not the cause. And I knew [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE] and lots kind of [INAUDIBLE]. I knew all those tricks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it [INAUDIBLE]. Did you try that salve or that salve or that salve? He said, no. Would it help? I said, perfectly. You do that. Keep your hands dry and never wash them until it dried up and heals. Thank you, my [INAUDIBLE], and thank you. You were a great help. Now, what is it I can do for you now? Why are you here? I said, listen. I have a problem. I, me, German lady, ja, and my family is lost. And I have to wait till they come. And I have to have an [GERMAN], the housing. And I don't have a pass. Would you be so kind to give me a pass, that I can stay in that and that address? No question. I mean, it was such [? great, ?] such a doctor like me, and a German as well. I mean, I got the pass. I grabbed the gun. I grabbed my old guard. I had the-- we were hopping, hopping, hopping back. And the mean guy was standing by the window. And I waived my passport from [? far. ?] He was-- And do you know what? I had my own room from then on. And soldiers were sleeping in front of the door so the bad guy shouldn't puzzle me anymore till they got deported.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we all Germans had to be deported to [? Hela, ?] Peninsula [? Hela. ?] That was the last-- the last place was still under Nazi, as far as I know, Peninsula [? Hela. ?] So they were there, lying there along the [INAUDIBLE], along that river. And the first SS came and went on that boat, whatever that is, then the officers. And then the soldiers came, and they couldn't take anything with them to have more space, and the women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't want to go. I mean, here, this is my chance. And I said, why don't you go ahead? I will wait for the next boat. Why don't you-- and next was the next ferry. And did I get compliments. Only a German woman can be so brave. Only a German woman is so selfless. Only a German woman can be anything I am. I am absolutely the [INAUDIBLE] and the [INAUDIBLE] all turned into one. They couldn't get [? where-- ?] they couldn't be grateful enough for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were lying [? there ?] as the Russians were bombing. And then it was over. They didn't have any more boats. And I went back to the-- and there were no Germans, no boats. [? Rolf, ?] from the office came over and said to me, Veronica, he said, learn something in life. Let everything [GERMAN]. Cope with everything in life which happens the best you can, and don't get scared. That [? was ?] his last words. He went down to Russian, to Siberia. He was a dentist, [GERMAN]. Anyhow, that was [? Rolf. ?] And, and that what he said. Veronica, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. OK. I did. And then I stayed in this house. And it was kind of tricky to get out, because the Russians were in town, and they were angry. And they, they raped and-- and-- and [? stole ?] things. And I mean, they were angry. And it was dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed all day in the house, at night, I came out at dawn and took water from the pump and washed myself, because washing myself was my ritual. Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. All my own, the whole house had to myself, my whole villa. And here I see the French prisoners of war, whom I remember from one of my camps. They were on the other side of the-- of the-- of the fence. Either it was Kaiserwald or Stutthof or the-- I don't know which camp-- marching by.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember some of the guys. There was [? Rene. ?] He always flew over things from the Red Cross. And there was another guy which always used to [? slow ?] the [INAUDIBLE]. And I kept on saying, hello, [? Rene. ?] He said, what are you do here? And I told them my-- my predicament. And he said, you are coming with us, [? Rene ?] said. [? Rene ?] was a wonderful [INAUDIBLE]. You are not staying here. You're coming with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they dressed me up. And my hair was-- [INAUDIBLE] cut a little more, the hair. They dressed me up in that uniform. And they got the tags on [INAUDIBLE]. And then we had to be in fives. So it taught me how to count French and to my number, when-- when I had to count the-- And then I walked with them all over Poland. They were going to the French zone. That was Stuttgart or [INAUDIBLE]. It was a French zone. Well, out from the Russian zone, they were going to the French zone. And I-- I walked with them. And I had to share a bed with [? Rene. ?] He was very decent. He watched over me like a daddy. And then we got on the-- on the train. We had to sit-- it was only-- only a place on the roof. So I, of course, I held onto [? Rene, ?] and then he held the arm around me. And we were just sitting there. And then when-- at one point, the train went on a bend. And it was slippery. It was raining, and it went slow. And I fell asleep, and-- and [? Rene ?] was asleep. And I fell off the train [INAUDIBLE] and turned my ankle. Ah. And I kept on yelling. And all the guys were saying, Alors, Jean, alors! I'm telling you, I ran after the train. I mean, Tom Cruise in didn't-- would have given me an A. I ran after the train with my [? limp ?] after the train, as all of the arms were down. And they dragged me up, and I [? called ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And was an [INAUDIBLE]. And they left me in Berlin. And they went to Stuttgart, and they left me in Berlin. The war was over at that point. They left me in a place, the German-- I think the kids can come-- [INAUDIBLE], I-- I met some people from my camp. I met Mike, the cook from Kaiserwald, who told me that his son got killed, got shot while try to escape, that young son of his. That [? Jacob ?] was shot, that [INAUDIBLE] was shot, that [INAUDIBLE] was shot. All of them were shot trying to escape. He was there all alone. I met people who were Jewish girls whom I knew from Kaiswerwald. There was a girl from Leipzig. And that was my choice. I had to either go back home. That was Russia, Latvia. And I didn't know if my parents or anybody is alive. Or I want to stay in the west. I mean, I had this idea about-- about the Dane. Maybe I stay Danish or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, [? Fanny ?] [INAUDIBLE] came from Leipzig. And she said to me, why don't you pretend coming from Leipzig, Germany? And in Leipzig, the Jews were in the fur business, which was called in German [GERMAN], which was a really an in name. I thought [GERMAN] has to do with smoking. So the guy was writing out, where are you from-- from Leipzig? What did your parents do? As I said, they [GERMAN], they had cigarettes to sell. Well, that gave me almost away. But he-- he-- he just winked at me, and I didn't have to go to-- back to the Russian zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And well, I go-- I have [INAUDIBLE] those ladies from-- from my camp, [? Fanny, ?] and [? Rosel ?] and [? Rosel's-- ?] I think [? Rosel's ?] husband showed up. And I got-- as they sent me off to the office, to [INAUDIBLE] office, [? I said ?] that was a guy from the commandant of Berlin, if I could get a pass to leave Berlin. We all walked back over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we met Americans, while we walked out, and met the Americans walking in Berlin. And do you know what my first how-do-you-do was as Americans? I hate to say it. You kikes are still around? I thought they got you all. And well, it was a [INAUDIBLE]. Let me-- I forgive it. I forgive them. I forgive them. I forgive them. Anyhow, that's what I said. But we met-- we made it into-- we hitchhiked, and made it into Stuttgart, which was a French zone. And there again, we had a house for us. And the [? Fanny ?] and I was there. And I got-- is [? it ?] this has to do with survival still? I got into trouble. There was a guy who was very strange looking and had a Swiss-- a Swiss cross here and a Swiss cross here and a Swiss cross here and a Swiss cross here, and always was talking German, and ran after us all the time. One day. He was with us. And one day, I am-- I walked down the street in my-- in my evening clothes and my jacket. And there were-- there were [? tenders, ?] I mean jeeps. And people, men were-- people were there, talked a strange language. It was familiar, but somehow it rang a bell. I didn't that-- [? big ?] Jewish [? star. ?] What's that? And I hang around. And those guys thought I am a prostitute, I'm soliciting. And they kept on throwing stones of me, German whore. And I said, I'm not a German whore. I happened to be Jewish. My-- And you are Jewish? Come on, prove it to me. I said, who was-- who was your [YIDDISH]? He said it in Yiddish. I said, [YIDDISH] was Chaim and Sophie Saks. But no, wrong. They wanted to say Abram, Isaak, and [INAUDIBLE]. [? Then they, ?] where are you from? I said, [INAUDIBLE], Latvia.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had one for there, from somebody was from there. And this guy came and he was-- knew my parents. So then that was the idea. I got organized. They took me on-- on, on, on a truck at night, over there, over [INAUDIBLE], over the-- over the mountains into Italy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this time, were you able to locate your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know [INAUDIBLE], no, not for a long time, not. In Italy, we went on [INAUDIBLE]. They were on a farm or I worked in a hospital. And there where my problem started. One day, there was a rumor that the German SS lady has escaped and is-- is about in Italy. And I go with my-- my checkered blue and white from work innocently over their plaza. And somebody says, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. They tried to-- but they were angry, those Jews. They tried to tear me into pieces. Then the American [INAUDIBLE], M-- M--

speaker: interviewer
text: MPs.

speaker: interviewee
text: MPs came and grabbed me, schlepped me into prison. Here I sit in the prison. Whom do I meet? This strange guy with the Swiss-- Swiss crosses, all bloodied, all smashed in. One eye cannot open. Then the teeth are missing, and [? he ?] sits there like a hunk of raw meat. And he says, ask her. She knows me. I know him? He said, the guy said, do you know this guy? I said, yes, he was in Stuttgart. He was in that same place we were. And he kept on walking after us all the time. What happened, he was one of the worst henchmen for Mauthausen. He just joined us and thought this way, he's gonna get away with it. And by pointing me out that I know him, I was in bad company. That was not a very good introduction. And I didn't know who he was. I said, of course, I know. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I said, do I have anybody who knows me? And I knew somebody from-- from that hospital that was in Riga, [INAUDIBLE] from Riga, and a lawyer. And Rabinowitz I think was his name-- or [? Rappaport, ?] [? Rappaport. ?] He came there and vouched for me. And I got out from that bloody prison. How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a day or night. It's nothing much. [? Rappaport ?] came soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to [? [INAUDIBLE]. ?] [? We ?] went to a wonderful farm outside from Milano, outside of Milano, [INAUDIBLE], which was owned by a Jewish-- a Jewish woman married to an Italian aristocrat. And she had us all there. And she tried to adopt me even. I said, why are you going to Palestine? Now you had such a hard time. I don't have children, and so on. I could have gone then I contacted my-- my uncle in Africa and my aunt. But I-- I didn't-- still didn't know about Mama. And they went back to [INAUDIBLE]. And then one day, they took us on a-- on the [INAUDIBLE]. I think they-- yes, [INAUDIBLE] is an illegal-- illegal ship to Israel. We just loaded the ship and loaded the ship with us. And this is about the thing which happened to me. And then I ended in a kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [INAUDIBLE]. At first, [INAUDIBLE], and then I get [INAUDIBLE]. And there I met [INAUDIBLE]. And then we went to his kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had the most glorious uniform. He was a [INAUDIBLE]. He had a big Australian hat. And it has-- [INAUDIBLE] in the war, they wore those watches which had leather covers [? the ?] up and down. That was fabulously sexy. You know, they had-- and they had those things that were covered so they shouldn't shine? [LAUGHS] I thought it was so sexy. [LAUGHS] Had this big Australian hat. And they had dogs. Well, we-- we really got on very well, had lots of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: His name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Emanuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kahn. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just-- who came to your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to hear about my wedding? Well, we-- I then moved to his kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], which was a little-- it was a different political thing. It was [INAUDIBLE], and [INAUDIBLE] was [INAUDIBLE]. I moved to that kibbutz, [INAUDIBLE]. I lived in a tent with our dog. And-- and then-- in those days, it was like that. You didn't get married before, before-- married was moving into a [INAUDIBLE], into a family room. That was considered officially married, even not with a rabbi, OK? But when you got pregnant, you had to go to the rabbi. But there was a condition to go to the rabbi. It had to be winter, when there has no work to do on the fields. You had to have more than one pregnant lady. It was during the war, mind you, more or less, during the war, '48. They had to be more pregnant, so it's worth the gasoline. Then you had to have a free truck. Then you had to go to the next kibbutz, where there was an old gentleman who was a rabbi. High and below, I was already pregnant, good pregnant. So was [? Ziva. ?] We had two. That was raining. There was nothing to do on the fields, had enough gasoline. We have gasoline. We were going to-- to [INAUDIBLE], to the rabbi. And [INAUDIBLE] Every time they saw girls with bellies, they were hiding in their rooms. And they didn't want to be a minyan. They wanted to rest after work. And they [LAUGHS] were hiding, locking up. Anyhow, our boys from our-- from our kibbutz, the young guys, were sitting there on the lawn. They were waiting for the order to go to the [INAUDIBLE]. There was-- they had the battles in the Jordan-- at Jordan, in the Jordan Valley. Their [INAUDIBLE] didn't have the orders. So they were happy to have something to do. There was on guy called [INAUDIBLE]. He was the clown of the group. He went the head of the procession and did ta, ta, ta-ra da, da, da. After that, I came with my belly after that. And then [INAUDIBLE] kind of my husband's [INAUDIBLE] little shape [INAUDIBLE]. Then came [? Ziva, ?] came with her belly and [? Eliezer ?] after her. We walked around the lawn, and we came down. And we got a chuppah, and we were standing there. And the whistle blows. And the [INAUDIBLE], and they jump on the track and went to the battle. And here we were standing there, the rabbi with a belly, [? Ziva ?] with a belly. I with a belly. [LAUGHS] Without a minyan. Now we had to call the guys, the people from the-- from the kibbutz to come out to make a minyan. And then we had to have a glass. Who had a glass in those days in '48. That's it, [? guys. ?]
